coredata.dc:description: Despite being the earliest and most extensively studied class of vitrimers, transesterification epoxy (EP) vitrimers still face some large limitations in thermomechanical stability and flammability. Unlike the preparation of functional composite materials using ordinary curing agent compounds and nano fillers, this paper proposed a new strategy of using carbon dots to design nano-curing agents and dynamically crosslink active sites for constructing epoxy vitrimers with excellent thermomechanical stability and flame retardancy. Herein carboxylic acid type carbon dots (C-CDs) was synthesized via glutamate pyrolysis, subsequently grafting with organophosphorus compounds via the Kabachnik-Fields reaction to achieve the phosphorus doping (P-CDs). It is interesting that the synthesized P-CDs played a quadruple role as flame retardants, curing agents, dynamically exchangeable bonds, and nano-reinforcement effect. The results showed that this new kind of organic-inorganic hybrid flame-retardant EP/CDs vitrimers was firstly reported, which exhibited good thermomechanical stability, flame retardancy and fast stress relaxation time. When the molar ratio of P-CDs to epoxy was about 1:1, the peak heat release rate (PHRR) and CO2 production (CO2P) of the EP/P-CDs could be reduced by 53.4 %, and 65.1 %. The limiting oxygen index (LOI) was 31.3 % and the UL-94 grade reached V-0 rating, which benefitted from the dual-phase flame-retardant mechanisms. Furthermore, the stress relaxation time of EP/P-CDs was significantly shortened to be 90 s, which was important for the reprocessing of epoxy thermosets. Notably, EP/P-CDs maintained up to 98 % of its thermo-mechanical properties even after twice reprocessing. This kind of organic-inorganic hybrid flame-retardant EP/CDs vitrimers will have imposing potential applications in many fields.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Despite being the earliest and most extensively studied class of vitrimers, transesterification epoxy (EP) vitrimers still face some large limitations in thermomechanical stability and flammability. Unlike the preparation of functional composite materials using ordinary curing agent compounds and nano fillers, this paper proposed a new strategy of using carbon dots to design nano-curing agents and dynamically crosslink active sites for constructing epoxy vitrimers with excellent thermomechanical stability and flame retardancy. Herein carboxylic acid type carbon dots (C-CDs) was synthesized via glutamate pyrolysis, subsequently grafting with organophosphorus compounds via the Kabachnik-Fields reaction to achieve the phosphorus doping (P-CDs). It is interesting that the synthesized P-CDs played a quadruple role as flame retardants, curing agents, dynamically exchangeable bonds, and nano-reinforcement effect. The results showed that this new kind of organic-inorganic hybrid flame-retardant EP/CDs vitrimers was firstly reported, which exhibited good thermomechanical stability, flame retardancy and fast stress relaxation time. When the molar ratio of P-CDs to epoxy was about 1:1, the peak heat release rate (PHRR) and CO2 production (CO2P) of the EP/P-CDs could be reduced by 53.4 %, and 65.1 %. The limiting oxygen index (LOI) was 31.3 % and the UL-94 grade reached V-0 rating, which benefitted from the dual-phase flame-retardant mechanisms. Furthermore, the stress relaxation time of EP/P-CDs was significantly shortened to be 90 s, which was important for the reprocessing of epoxy thermosets. Notably, EP/P-CDs maintained up to 98 % of its thermo-mechanical properties even after twice reprocessing. This kind of organic-inorganic hybrid flame-retardant EP/CDs vitrimers will have imposing potential applications in many fields.

Carboxylic-acid type carbon dots was used as nano-curing agent in constructing organic-inorganic hybrid epoxy vitrimers with excellent thermo-mechanical stability, high flame retardancy and good reprocessing performance.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of ester-exchange EP vitrimers typically involves the ring-opening reaction of epoxy groups in EP with acids or anhydrides [1–3], resulting in the formation of ester groups and vicinal hydroxyl groups. Transesterification epoxy (EP) vitrimers is regarded as the first and most common type of vitrimers. Upon heating, these hydroxyl groups undergo reversible exchange reactions with the ester groups, generating new hydroxyl and ester groups. This materials class was first introduced by Leibler's group [4], who employed zinc acetate as a catalyst to crosslink epoxy groups with fatty acids. This process facilitates the rearrangement of the topology network of the material at high temperatures, resulting in exceptional reprocessability. EP vitrimers retain the mechanical properties of thermosetting materials while enabling reprocessing and recyclability [5,6]. However, EP vitrimers still suffer from poor thermal stability and inadequate flame retardancy, which significantly limit their applications. Currently, extensive research has been conducted on flame-retardant EP vitrimers. These approaches primarily enhance flame retardant properties through the introduction of phosphate ester groups, which significantly improve the material's resistance to fire [7–12].

Carbon dots (CDs) are zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials typically smaller than 10 nm in size [13,14]. Due to their diverse synthesis methods (top-down and bottom-up) [15] and a broad range of precursors (such as organic small molecules, graphite, etc.), CDs have experienced rapid advancements in research. Recent studies have focused on the application of CDs in flame retardancy. Zhang et al. [16] synthesized nitrogen-phosphorus doped CDs (N, P-CDs) using citric acid as the carbon source and hexachlorotripolyphosphonitrile as the nitrogen and phosphorus source. These N, P-CDs were then blended with ammonium polyphosphate (APP) to create a synergistic P–N flame retardant system (SPFRS). This SPFRS was then incorporated into the EP matrix to improve the flame-retardant properties of the EP. The resulting flame-retardant EP composite (EP/CQD-APP2:18) achieved a UL-94 V-0 rating and an LOI value of 33.2 %. However, the conventional approach of adding CDs as additives into the matrix may lead to rendezvous issues. As an alternative, introducing CDs as intrinsic crosslinkers within the matrix may help overcome this limitation and improve dispersion.

Achieving uniform dispersion of nanoparticles within polymeric resins remains a persistent challenge, impeding the practical utilization of polymer composites containing nanoparticles. The surface of CDs is rich in various functional groups, such as carboxyl, amino, and hydroxyl groups [17], which facilitates surface modification (with organic molecules or polymers) and enables effective crosslinking and solidification with matrix. For instance, Lee et al. [18] demonstrated the successful solidification of an EP using solvent-thermally synthesized, amine-rich CDs prepared from o-phenylenediamine, m-phenylenediamine, and p-phenylenediamine. Similarly, CDs containing both amino and carboxyl groups were obtained via microwave carbonization of citric acid and urea, and these CDs were crosslinked with polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) [19] to form a hydrogel. As crosslinking agents, CDs not only have excellent compatibility with polymer matrices but also can strengthen both the optical properties and thermal stability of the materials. In addition, the synthesis of CDs is simple, easy, and non-toxic. However, the application of CD as a crosslinking or curing agent in epoxy composite materials has not been reported yet.

Herein, the preparation and performance of processable luminescent EP vitrimers using carboxylic acid CDs as curing agents to cure epoxy monomer (DER332) and transesterification agent to construct dynamic exchangeable bonds in the crosslinking network were proposed for the first time in this work. Carboxyl-functionalized carbon dots (C-CDs) and DOPO-grafted phosphorus-doped carbon dots (P-CDs) were synthesized from glutamate precursors via a pyrolysis approach. The as-prepared EP/CDs vitrimers exhibited excellent thermal-mechanical stability and flame retardancy as well as reprocessability. The abundant carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs served as active sites for EP curing, facilitating strong reactivity and uniform dispersion of the CDs within the matrix, which enhanced the mechanical properties. EP/CDs vitrimers displayed outstanding thermomechanical stability both before and after reshaping. This work paves the avenues for constructing organic-inorganic hybrid EP/CDs vitrimers composites.

Bisphenol A diglycidyl ether (DER332) was purchased from Dow Chemical Company, US; glutamate (Glu, 99 %), polyoxymethylene (POM, 95 %), 1-methylimidazole (1-MI, 99 %) were all obtained from Aladdin. 9,10-dihydro-9-oxa-10-phosphaphenanthrene-10-oxide (DOPO, 97 %) was purchased from Macklin. Ethanol, tetrahydrofuran (THF), and other chemical reagents were supplied by Xi Long Scientific.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of two co-curing agents: carboxyl-functionalized carbon dots (C-CDs) and DOPO-grafted phosphorus-doped carbon dots (P-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: C-CDs: Glutamate (2 g) was placed in a 50 mL single-necked flask and pyrolyzed [20] at 210 °C for 20 min. During the process, the solution transitioned from colorless and transparent to yellow, indicating the initial synthesis of CDs. After it had cooled to ambient temperature, 20 mL of water was added to disperse the product. The resulting mixture was then centrifuged at 11,000 rpm for 10 min and filtered through a 0.22 μm water filterable membrane. Finally, the product was freeze-dried, yielding carboxyl-functionalized carbon dots (C-CDs).

P-CDs: C-CDs (1 g) was dispersed in chloroform using ultrasonication. Subsequently, DOPO (2.9 g, 0.013 mol) and POM (0.41 g, 0.013 mol) were then added to the dispersion. The dispersion was heated to 60 °C and refluxed for 24 h under nitrogen protection. At the end of the reaction, the solvent was removed from the obtained solution by rotary evaporation and a white powder was obtained. The product was then washed three times with acetonitrile to remove any unreacted DOPO. followed by three additional washes with water. Finally, the product was freeze-dried for 24 h to obtain DOPO-grafted phosphorus-doped carbon dots (P-CDs) [21]. The chemical structures of C-CDs and P-CDs are schematically illustrated in Fig. 1 (b) .

Molar quantification of the carboxyl group on CDs: Back titration was employed to determine the carboxyl group content [22] on the CDs. A solution of CDs at a concentration of X g/mL was prepared in V1 mL. A known excess volume (V2 mL) of 0.1 M NaOH solution was added to each CD solution, with phenolphthalein as an indicator. The standard NaOH was then titrated with a standard 0.1 M HCl solution until the pink color disappeared, indicating the endpoint. The volume of HCl used in the titration was recorded as V3. This titration was repeated three times to ensure accuracy. The amount of carboxyl groups on the CDs (mol/g) was calculated using formula (1):

The molar concentrations of carboxylic acid in C-CDs and P-CDs were determined to be 0.0057 mol/g and 0.0037 mol/g, respectively.

The testing of the phosphorus content of P-CDs was conducted on Agilent ICP-OES 5110. The results showed that the P content of P-CDs was 14.14 % ± 0.06 %. In order to ensure the complete grafting of DOPO into the surface of the carbon dot, the excess feed of DOPO was necessary. After finishing the reaction, the redundant DOPO could be washed away.

Based on the recipes listed above Table 1 , a range of EP/CDs vitrimers composites were prepared via a curing procedure. The curing agents of carbon dots such as C-CDs or P-CDs were dispersed in ethanol by ultrasonication and then added to EP. The mixture was stirred uniformly at 70 °C to obtain a transparent solution. To ensure the even dispersion of the curing agents within the substrate, the mixture was sonicated for an additional 30 min. After removing the ethanol and bubbles through vacuum heating, 1-MI (1 wt% of EP) was added as the curing accelerator and mixed for 3 min to obtain an epoxy prepolymer which was poured into a warm-up Teflon cast and cured in an oven according to a curing program of 130 °C/2 h, 160 °C/4 h and 180 °C/2 h. For comparison, Here, EP/C-CDs were prepared as a control experiment for comparison.

The gel fraction of EP/CDs vitrimers was measured using the immersion method. The sample (W1, approximately 0.5 g) was immersed in 20 mL of THF for 24 h at room temperature. After wiping the surface with solvent, the mass was weighed as W2, and the insoluble fraction was dried to a settled weight (W3) in an oven at 70 °C for 12 h. The gel fraction (GF) and swelling ratio (SR) of the sample were calculated using the following formulas (2) and (3):

The C-CDs and P-CDs were characterized using FT-IR, NMR, TEM, ultraviolet absorption spectroscopy, and photoluminescence (PL) analysis. The FT-IR spectra of C-CDs, DOPO and P-CDs are presented in Fig. 2 (a) . The broad peaks at 3200–3450 cm−1 in C-CDs were assigned to the stretching vibrations of O–H and amino N–H groups. The peak at 1700 cm−1 was assigned to CO of carboxylic acid. In the DOPO, the P–H bond was observed at 2436 cm−1 [23], which disappeared in the P-CDs, suggesting that DOPO was fully engaged in the reaction. The CO stretching of C-CDs was retained at 1710 cm−1 in the P-CDs, while new stretching vibrational peaks for Ph-O-P appeared at 928 cm−1. Furthermore, peaks at 1199 cm−1 and 1581 cm−1 corresponded to the carboxyl groups in the DOPO structure, indicating the presence of PO and P-Ph vibrations [24]. These results confirmed the successful reaction of DOPO with C-CDs.

The 1H NMR spectra in Fig. 2 (b) showed that the signals between 1.7–2.4 ppm corresponded to the C–H protons of the alkane groups on the surface of the CDs [25]. The signal at 8.81 ppm was affiliated to the P–H proton in DOPO [26]. Notably, the P–H signal disappeared in the P-CDs compared to DOPO, while a new N–H signal peak appeared at 5.53 ppm in the P-CDs [27]. Besides, new signals at about 4.15 ppm were assigned to the methylene protons of -N–CH2–DOPO, indicating that the amino group has reacted with DOPO. The partially enlarged 1H NMR spectrum of C-CDs was shown in Fig. S1, and the proton of the carboxyl group could be observed at 12.45 ppm (Fig. S1). In the 31P NMR spectrum (Fig. 2(c) and Figure S(4–5)), DOPO had a characteristic signal near 16.03 ppm and 13.00 ppm, while the P-CDs showed a single peak at 33.51 ppm, which reflected the different spatial environments of DOPO before and after the reaction. It is worth noting that the multiple peaks in 31P NMR of DOPO is mainly caused by the reason that the steric hindrance of the two large-volume side groups of the benzene ring, which leads to the formation of stereoisomers with unequal phosphorus. The 31P NMR results further confirmed the successful preparation of P-CDs.

TEM images in Fig. 2 (d) revealed that the average particle size of C-CDs and P-CDs was 2.0 nm and 2.2 nm, respectively (Figure S6 (a)), with a highly ordered crystalline structure characterized by lattice parameters of 0.26 nm [28]. Besides, it was observed that both the C-CDs and P-CDs solution displayed significant blue luminescence under 365 nm UV light irradiation. Fig. 2(e and f) illustrates the UV absorption, PL emission, and excitation spectra of C-CDs and P-CDs. The ultraviolet absorption of C-CDs was at 210 nm and 326 nm, which were attributed to n–π∗ transitions of CC and CO respectively. Compared with C-CDs, the absorption peaks of P-CDs shifted to 207 nm and 290 nm [28]. Additionally, a new absorption peak at 264 nm was observed, which was assigned to DOPO. Correspondingly, the PL spectra showed blue-shifted emission (from 440 nm to 375 nm) and excitation (from 360 nm to 330 nm), primarily due to the transformation of electron-donating amino groups to amide bonds, which reduced the π-π∗ conjugated electron density [29].

The curing behaviors of the EP/CDs vitrimers were studied using differential scanning calorimetry (DSC). The CDs were readily mixed with the liquid phase epoxy monomer. Fig. 3 (a) showed the DSC curves tested at a heating rate (β) of 10 K/min. Both EP/C-CDs and EP/P-CDs exhibited exothermic curing peaks (Tp), indicating strong reactivity with the substrate. The Tp value of EP/C-CDs1 (126.93 °C) was significantly lower than that of EP/P-CDs (155.29–158.24 °C) with different molar ratios of carboxyl to epoxy groups (x = 0.50, 0.75, 1.00 and 1.25). This difference can be owing to the steric hindrance effect of DOPO, which restricts the chain mobility of EP/P-CDs1 [30,31].

Figure S7 (a-b) showed the dynamic DSC curves of EP cured with C-CDs1 and P-CDs1 at different heating rates (5, 10, 15 and 20 K/min). The curing activation energy (Ea) for the curing process was defined via the Kissinger method [32], as shown in formula (4):
(4)

The activation energy for curing (Ea), can be calculated using the gas constant R (8.314 J mol−1 K−1) and the plot of ln (β/Tp 2) versus 1000/Tp, as shown in Fig. 3 (b). The DSC test results can be found in Table S1. The curing Ea values of EP/C-CDs1 and EP/P-CDs1 were 64.7 kJ/mol and 75.3 kJ/mol, respectively, which fall within the range of 43.35–105.27 kJ/mol [33,34].

It is known that the complete curing of epoxy resin is a basic requirement for the EP thermoset material. Herein, the DSC of cured EP/C-CDs1 and EP/P-CDs1 were conducted in two cycles and shown in Fig. S8 The results showed that no exothermic peaks were generated during the whole temperature range, which proved that the set curing procedure condition was appropriate for the preparation of completely cured EP/C-CDs1 and EP/P-CDs1 vitrimer in this work. The TEM images (Fig. 3(c and d)) revealed that EP/C-CDs1 and EP/P-CDs1 exhibited excellent molecular-level compatibility with the EP monomer prior to the curing reaction, which was essential for achieving desirable mechanical and flame-retardant properties in the resulting polymer nanocomposites [35]. However, significant agglomeration was observed in the EP/P-CDs1.25 (Fig. 3 (e)) formulation. To ensure a complete substrate reaction with CDs and to maintain the validity of the test data, EP/P-CDs 1.25 was excluded from the performance evaluation. FT-IR analysis (Figure S7 (c)) confirmed the successful reaction between the epoxy and the carboxyl group, as evidenced by the vanish of the epoxy group peak at 910 cm−1 in the cured DER332. The emergence of the ester group stretching vibration at 1730 cm−1 and the hydroxyl group peak near 3400 cm−1 further verified the successful reaction [36].

Stress relaxation characterization is a powerful technique for monitoring dynamic bond exchange reactions. In the presence of a transesterification agent (1-MI) and elevated temperatures, hydroxyl and ester groups in the EP/C-CDs and EP/P-CDs polymer networks undergo bond exchange reactions, facilitating the rearrangement of polymer topology. The stress relaxation behavior of EP/CDs vitrimers was measured using DMA850, where the relaxation time (τ) is deemed to be the duration for the relaxation stress or modulus (G) to decrease to 0.37 of its original value [37]. A smaller τ indicated a faster dynamic bond exchange rate. As shown in Fig. 4 (a) , the stress relaxation curves of EP/CDs vitrimers at 160 °C under a linear strain of 0.05 % weree presented. The τ values for EP/C-CDs1, EP/P-CDs0.5, EP/P-CDs0.75 and EP/P-CDs1 were 180 s, 322 s, 237 s and 166 s, respectively. Notably, as the content of P-CDs increased, τ for the EP/P-CDs system gradually decreased, which could be attributed to a higher concentration of transesterification bonds that enhanced the exchange probability of dynamic bonds [38]. Moreover, the τ value of EP/P-CDs1 was much shorter than that of EP/CDs vitrimers previously reported in the literatures [39–47], Among these previous works, the organic acids were chosen as curing agents (Fig. 4 (d)). This phenomenon could be explained that, the CDs nano-curing agent in the cross-linker network and the existence of benzene rings in DOPO increased the free volume of the epoxy chain, resulting in the enhancement of the flexibility of the macromolecular chains. On the other hand, the large surface area of CDs brought a platform of ester bonds with high-density, facilitating the migration and rearrangement of those bonds, which benefited the transesterification of epoxy vitrimers [48].

Given that EP/P-CDs1 exhibited the fastest stress relaxation rate among these samples, the stress relaxation dynamic behaviors of EP/P-CDs1 at different temperatures were further investigated. Fig. 4 (b) showed that the τ value for EP/P-CDs1 decreases from 240 s at 150 °C to 90 s at 170 °C, illustrating an accelerated rate of transesterification at elevated temperatures. The activation energy (Ea) of the transesterification reaction was determined using the Arrhenius equation (5) [49]:
(5)
ln
τ =
E a
R T
− ln
A
Where R represents the gas constant (8.314 J mol−1 K−1), A is the pre-exponential factor and T is the thermodynamic temperature. Fig. 4 (c) showed the calculated Ea value for EP/P-CDs as 75.44 kJ/mol, which falls within the reported range of 69–150 kJ/mol [43,50] for transesterification documented by other research groups.

The topology freezing transition temperature (T v ) is a crucial parameter for vitrimers [51], defined as the temperature at which a dynamic network transitions from a solid to a viscoelastic liquid state due to bond-swapping reactions [52]. To determine T v , thermal expansion measurements were used to generate the thermal expansion coefficient versus the temperature curve. This method applies to materials that exhibit significant volumetric changes in thermal expansion behavior near the transition temperature. The procedure was illustrated in Figure S9 (a-d). The T v values for the EP/C-CDs1 and EP/P-CDs systems were 157.8 °C, 154.1 °C, 152.4 °C and 151.6 °C, respectively. The coefficient of thermal expansion of the material increases significantly near T v , which is likely due to thermally activated ester exchange reactions [53].

The reprocessing temperature of vitrimers is decided by the relative values of the glass transition temperature (Tg) and T v , and it must exceed both transition temperatures to enable effective reprocessing. By comparing the T v measured using the thermal expansion method and the Tg measured using DMA, the reprocessing temperature of the EP vitrimer should be higher than the former (157.8 °C). To improve the reshaping rate, the crushed EP/P-CDs1 was subjected to hot compression at 200 °C and 10 MPa for 3 h, successfully reshaping into a complete film (Fig. 4 (e)). However, it was seen that the hot-pressing operation temperature was higher than the topology transition temperature (Tv) and the glass transition temperature (Tg), which could simultaneously cause the thermo-oxidation problem for the epoxy vitrimer, resulting in the darker samples. The chemical characterization of the recycled materials by FT-IR and UV–vis spectroscopy was shown in Fig. S10. It was seen from the UV–vis spectra that with the increase of reprocessing times, the transmittance of EP/P-CDs1 showed a decreasing trend. The FT-IR showed that the stretching vibration peak of the corresponding hydroxyl group at 3100 - 3700 cm−1 showed a decreasing trend after reprocessing. Correspondingly, the FT-IR peak intensity of the ester group near 1735 cm−1 showed an increasing trend, and the ester group showed a trend of moving towards lower wavenumbers of about 1720 cm−1. This might be due to the oxidation reaction of hydroxyl groups and the breakage reaction of the ester group, forming more carboxyl groups during hot-pressing, which corresponded to the yellowing even dark phenomena.

The film emitted blue fluorescence under 365 nm ultraviolet irradiation and retained good transparency even after three cycles of hot-pressing (Fig. 5 (c) ). The FT-IR (Fig. 5 (a)) and PL (Fig. 5 (b)) spectra of the reshaped sample showed almost no changes, indicating that EP/P-CDs1 possesses robust reprocessing capability. Meanwhile, the self-healing characteristics of EP/P-CDs1 were observed under an optical microscope [54]. A cross pattern with an initial scratch width of 93.93 μm was etched onto the surface of the sample, which reduced to 10.65 μm after 10 min (88.7 % self-healing rate) and further to 5.65 μm after 20 min (94 % self-healing rate) of heating at 180 °C, demonstrating its superior self-healing performance (Fig. 5 (d)). The welding behavior of EP/P-CDs1 was depicted in Fig. 5 (e). By overlapping the ends of two vitrimers samples by 2 cm and heating at 180 °C for 2 h, the welded piece was able to withstand a weight of 100 g without detachment, highlighting its remarkable weldability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal stability and flame retardancy of EP/CDs vitrimers
ce:section.ce:para: The thermal stability property of glutamic acid carbon dots C-CDs and DOPO-modified carbon dots P-CDs have been characterized by TGA under N2 atmosphere. The relevant data was listed in Table S2 and Fig. S11a). It showed that the initial decomposition temperature (T5%) of C-CDs and P-CDs was 243.0 °C and 240.8 °C. The maximum decomposition temperature (T50 %) of C-CDs and P-CDs was 321.1 °C and 307.2 °C respectively. It was found that the degradation temperature of P-CDs decreased, which was explained by the reason that the surface modification of C-CDs by DOPO was successful and the thermal degradation of P-CDs was affected by the grafting DOPO. The degradation temperature of the organic compound DOPO was about 208.7 °C, which was lower than C-CDs. Besides, DOPO degraded completely at 800 °C with only 0.04 % residual char. Therefore, compared with C-CDs, the thermal decomposition stability of P-CDs decreased and the char residue greatly reduced from 22.9 % to 0.90 % after the surface modification.

The thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) of the EP/CDs vitrimers were conducted under N2 (Fig. 6 (a) ) and air (Figure S11 (b-c)) atmosphere, indicating that the materials exhibit good thermal stability. These measurements were performed to highlight the effects of CDs and organophosphorus additives on the pyrolytic degradation and oxidative decomposition of EP substrate. Detailed data were summarized in Table S2. The initial thermal decomposition temperature (T5%), 50 % degradation temperature (T50 %) and maximum destruction temperature (Tmax) of the EP/P-CDs system were generally lower compared to those of EP/C-CDs1. The P–O–C bond in P-CD has a lower thermal stability compared to the C–C bond, which leads to the premature decomposition of EP/P-CD [30]. This early decomposition of the organophosphorus component catalyzed the production of a carbon layer, which served as an insulating layer against thermal effects. Additionally, the char residue of mass of char residue of EP/P-CDs1 800 °C increased from 11.78 % to 17.92 % in N2 atmosphere and from 0.03 % to 0.80 % in air atmosphere, indicating that P-CDs exhibit a catalytic carbonization effect.

Figure S11 (d) showed the weight loss curves of the original samples,1st reprocessed EP/P-CDs1 (1st re-EP/P-CDs1) and 2nd reprocessed EP/P-CDs1 (2nd re-EP/P-CDs1). Compared to the T5% of EP/P-CD1 (349.2 °C), the 1st re-EP/P-CDs1 and 2nd re-EP/P-CDs1 increased to 358.8 °C and 360.4 °C, respectively. This improvement in thermal stability was likely due to additional thermal cross-linking of EP/P-CD1 induced by heating exposure during reprocessing. At 800 °C, the residual carbon of EP/P-CDs1 (17.92 %),1st re-EP/P-CDs1 (17.98 %) and 2nd re-EP/P-CDs1 (17.97 %) remained barely changed, indicating excellent reprocessing capability without compromising thermal stability.

The flame retardancy of EP/CDs vitrimers was assessed using the LOI (Fig. 6 (b)) and UL-94 tests (Table S3). EP/C-CDs1 exhibited a mere LOI value of 25.1 % and did not achieve any flame retardant classification. Conversely, incorporating the DOPO precursor into the EP/P-CDs system significantly improved the flame retardancy of the material. Furthermore, as the curing agent content increased, the LOI value rose from 26.3 % to 31.3 %. Notably, EP/DOPO-CDs1 self-extinguished within 10 s after ignition and attained a V-0 rating, demonstrating excellent flame retardancy.

To further evaluate the heat and smoke release of EP/CDs vitrimers during burning, cone calorimetric test (CCT) tests were conducted [55]. The curves for heat release rate (HRR), total heat release (THR), total smoke production (TSP) and carbon dioxide production (CO2P) were displayed in Fig. 6(c–f) and the corresponding data were listed in Table S4. The ignition time (TTI) of EP/C-CDs1 was110 s, while the TTI values for EP/P-CDs0.5 and EP/P-CDs1 decreased to 86 s and 79 s, respectively, aligning with the decreased T5% and Tmax values observed in TGA. Furthermore, compared to EP/C-CDs1, EP/P-CDs1 demonstrated a notable 53.4 % reduction in pHRR (Fig. 6 (c)) and 64.4 % in THR (Fig. 6 (d)). In the EP/P-CDs system, the early decomposition of DOPO at 360 °C produced phosphorous-containing free radicals (P–O–C, P–O–C, and PO), as confirmed by TGA-FTIR (Fig. 7 (a, a'-c, c')). These radicals effectively quenched combustible hydrogen (•H) and hydroxyl (•HO) free radicals, promoting their conversion into a stable carbon layer. Consequently, this process reduced TSP and CO2P, thereby suppressing smoke generation and enhancing flame retardancy. Compared to EP/C-CDs1, EP/P-CDs1 exhibited a notable reduction of TSP (22 m2 vs 25 m2) (Fig. 6 (e)) and 65.1 % in CO2P (Fig. 6 (f)), while a slight increase in the average CO production (COP) were observed [56]. As shown in Fig. 6 (g), EP/P-CDs1 reached a UL-94 V-0 rating.

In addition, the difference between the original sample and the reprocessed sample were also compared to explore whether the reprocessing affected the flame retardancy. It was found that the CTT testing results of EP/P-CDs1 and 1st re-EP/P-CDs1 were almost the same, such as TTI (79 s vs 79 s), PHRR (531 kW m−2 vs 531 kW m−2), THR (29.4 MJ m−2 vs 29.4 MJ m−2), TSP (22 m2 vs 22 m2), COP (0.027 g/s vs 0.034 g/s) and CO2P (0.24 g/s vs 0.21 g/s). It was concluded that the flame-retardancy of EP/P-CDs1 was effectively retained after reprocessing.

TGA-FTIR analysis revealed that the total gas production (GS) curve (Fig. 7 (d)), as well as the emissions of CO (Fig. 7 (e)) and CO2 (Fig. 7 (f)) were significantly reduced. This reduction was chiefly attributed to CO, a byproduct of incomplete combustion during oxidative decomposition, thereby emphasizing the attainment of gas-phase flame retardancy mechanism [57]. The lower intensity of pyrolysis product can also be explained by the formation of a denser carbon layer, which acts as a robust barrier to hinder the release of pyrolysis products, resulting in a lower pHRR, as corroborated by the CCT results [58].

The flame retardancy observed in CCT tests is closely associated with the material's ability to emerge a stable carbon layer. The morphology and graphitization degree of CCT carbon residue were characterized using SEM (Fig. 8 (a, a'-c, c')) and Raman spectroscopy (Fig. 8(e–g)), respectively. The outer surface of the EP/C-CDs1 carbon residue was found to be loose and porous, which is ineffective in preventing the release of internal heat, leading to a high HRR. However, with an increased dosage of P-CDs curing agent, the outer surface of the carbon layer became progressively denser. This observation provides direct evidence of phosphorus's catalytic capability to promote the formation of a compact physical barrier carbon layer to impede further epoxy matrix decomposition [59]. Comparatively, the inner surface of EP/P-CDs1 exhibited smaller and denser pores than that of EP/C-CDs1, which along with the gas phase action and the condensation of the dense surface carbon layer, synergistically enhanced its flame retardancy. Furthermore, energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) mapping (Fig. 8 (d-d')) of EP/P-CDS1 showed the distribution of N and P elements. Raman analysis examined the graphitization degree of carbon residue, showing two characteristic peaks at 1350 cm−1 (D peak) and 1584 cm−1 (G peak), which correspond to disordered graphite and ordered graphite structures, respectively. The integrated intensity ratio (ID/IG) of these peaks reflected the orderliness of the carbon structure, with lower values indicating higher graphitization degrees [60]. The results demonstrated that EP/P-CDs0.5 and EP/P-CDs1 had lower ID/IG values (2.58 and 3.04) compared to EP/C-CDs1 (3.43). This reduction in ID/IG values reflects a higher graphitization in EP/P-CDs1, resulting in a more continuous and compact carbonized structure, consistent with the flame retardant properties observed in previous tests and SEM analysis.

The DSC curves and DMA results for EP/CDs vitrimers were depicted in Fig. 9 (a–c), and the relevant information were summarized in Table S5. Tg of the EP/CDs vitrimers was tested by DSC (Fig. 9 (a)). The data indicated that EP/C-CDs1 exhibits the highest Tg at 122.8 °C, while EP/P-CDs1 has the lowest Tg at 96.4 °C. The decreasing Tg values for EP/P-CDs with the increasing P-CDs amount may be attributed to the incorporation of DOPO, whose larger steric hindrance lowers the crosslink density of the EP/P-CDs network, resulting in a reduced Tg [61]. The storage modulus (E′) and loss factor (tan δ) were shown in Fig. 9(b and c). The lowest E′ value of 1672 MPa was obtained for EP/C-CDs1, while the E′ value of EP/P-CDs series increased with higher P-CDs content, reaching a maximum of 2104 MPa for EP/P-CDs1. This increase may be attributed to the specific molecular structure of DOPO, which enhances the E′ value of EP/P-CDs. The decreasing trend in Tg values for the EP/CDs vitrimers, as indicated by the DMA results, aligns well with the DSC findings. Additionally, the crosslink density of EP/CDs vitrimers was calculated based on rubber elasticity theory [62].
(6)

E ′
3 R T
Where T is the absolute temperature at Tg+30 °C, E′is the storage modulus at Tg+30 °C and R is the universal gas constant. The EP/CDs vitrimers exhibited a decreasing trend in crosslink density, primarily due to the incorporation of rigid DOPO groups, which reduced the curing activity and subsequently decreased the overall crosslink density [63].

The E′ for the original EP/P-CDs1 sample, the first and second reprocessed EP/P-CDs1 samples (Fig. 9(b and c)) was 2104.5 MPa, 2113.4 MPa and 2057.5 MPa, respectively. Apparently, the reprocessing procedure did not damage the epoxy resin structure and did not make their storage module deteriorate. Nevertheless, with successive remolding, the Tg of the material gradually rose from the initial 105.6 °C to 108.8 °C (1st re-EP/P-CDs1) and further to 125.1 °C (2nd re-EP/P-CDs1). This increase was likely due to additional thermal exposure during the hot-pressing process, which promoted further thermal crosslinking and led to an increase in Tg and ρ (2.67 and 2.64 vs 2.52). Additionally, the tensile stress-strain curves (Fig. 9 (f)) showed minimal changes before and after hot pressing. In summary, these findings indicated that the 2nd re-EP/P-CDs1 prepared using P-CDs curing effectively maintained the thermal, mechanical, and flame retardant properties of the original material properties, even after two reprocessing cycles. In addition, more reprocessing cycles beyond 3 times has also been conducted and the results were shown in Fig. S12. It was found that after hot pressing for three times, the tensile strength of 3rd re-EP/P-CDs1 decreased from 35.34 MPa of 2nd re-EP/P-CDs1 to 10.5 MPa with a 70 % decreasing. After 10 hot pressing cycles, the EP/P-CDs1 was damaged seriously to present an incomplete film without a detectable state for valuing the mechanical properties.

The results of three-point bending, tensile, and impact experiments for EP/CDs vitrimers were shown in Fig. 9(d and e), with the detailed information provided in Table S5. EP/P-CDs1 exhibited the highest flexural modulus (3252.1 MPa), surpassing that of EP/C-CDs1 (2513.9 MPa). It was interesting that compared to our previously reported epoxy resin cured system based on methyltetrahydrophthalic anhydride (METHPA) [64] and other research [65], the mechanical properties such as storage modulus and flexural modulus of the EP/CDs cured system were all enhanced, implying that the new CDs nano-curing agent played a role of nano-enforcement effect. The good tensile strength and impact strength of EP/CDs vitrimers could be explained by the reason that the presence of CDs curing agents provided additional free volume, which helped to absorb and dissipate energy, contributing to improved mechanical performance. The EDS mapping of the EP/C-CDs1 and EP/P-CDs1 vitrimer was shown in Fig. S13. The results showed that the phosphorus contents of EP/C-CDs1 and EP/P-CDs1 was 0 % and 0.92 % respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Aging property and solvent resistance of EP/P-CDs vitrimers
ce:section.ce:para: The thermo-oxidative aging of the cured EP/P-CDs1 vitrimer at 100 °C and 200 °C for 24 h was performed and shown in Fig. 10 . When the sample was exposed in air atmosphere or vacuum conditions, the corresponding thermo-oxidation change was different, which was characterized by FT-IR and UV–vis spectroscopy. The experimental results showed that the aging process was mainly dominated by temperature. Under 200 °C for 24 h, the EP/P-CDs1 showed significant yellowing regardless of whether it was in air or vacuum environment. However, the yellowing was much slighter under 100 °C. The FT-IR characterization showed that after the thermo-oxidative procedure, the intensity of the –OH absorption peak at ∼3350 cm−1 decreased, meanwhile, the absorption peak at 1735 cm−1 corresponding to –CO(O) greatly increased, which proved the thermo-oxidative aging. Besides, the characteristic peak of the ester group shifted from 1735 cm−1 to a lower wavenumber of ∼1720 cm−1, which meant the breaking of –CO(O) to –CO‧- during the thermo-oxidation. Moreover, the asymmetric stretching vibration absorption of -C-O-CO at 1239 cm−1 also moved to the lower location at ∼1232 cm−1, verifying the breakage of –CO(O) due to aging. Among these samples, the sequence of the variation degree was EP/P-CDs1-air-200 °C > EP/P-CDs1-vacuum-200 °C > EP/P-CDs1-air-100 °C. The results of UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy also showed the same change trend. In a word, as the temperature increased, the degree of aging increased. At the same temperature, the presence of oxygen accelerated its aging degree.

We have also tested the solvent stability of the EP/P-CDs vitrimer by soaking it in different solvents for 48 h at room temperature. Nine solvents including organic solvent and water were chosen for the testing. They were tetrahydrofuran (THF), N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), methanol (MeOH), water (H2O), trichloromethane (CHCl3), acetone (ACE), toluene (TOL), and acetonitrile (ACN). It could be seen from Figure S14 that the sequence of solvent resistance of EP/P-CDs was estimated to be DMF < MeOH ≈ CHCl3 ≈ ACE ≈ H2O < THF ≈ DMSO ≈ TOL ≈ ACN. In addition, the water contact angle of EP composited vitrimers was tested and the relevant data were shown in Fig. 10(i). It could be seen that all the EP vitrimers exhibited hydrophobic properties with a contact angle of 103.5°, 94.5°, 105.4° and 108.9° for EP/C-CDs1, EP/P-CDs0.5, EP/P-CDs0.75 and EP/P-CDs1, respectively. The results indicated that the addition of carbon dots could improve the hydrophobicity.

In conclusion, carboxylic acid carbon dots (C-CDs) and DOPO-grafted carboxylic acid carbon dots (P-CDs) were successfully synthesized, and EP/CDs transesterification vitrimers (EP/C-CDs and EP/P-CDs) were subsequently fabricated. In this work, carbon dots were used as a new type of curing agent with nano-enhancing effects to replace common curing agents. The plentiful carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs serve as active sites for the curing of EP monomers, enhancing the reactivity of CDs and ensuring their uniform dispersion within the EP matrix. The forming ester bonds provided dynamic crosslinking active sites and made EP/CDs generate fast stress relaxation, which endowed the EP/CDs vitrimers with good reprocessing ability through dynamic bond exchange. For example, the relaxation time of EP/P-CDs1 at 170 °C was merely 90 s. The using of CDs as curing agents could play a nano-reinforcement effect, resulting in the improvement of the mechanical properties. In addition, the phosphorus-doped carbon dots greatly increased the flame retardancy. The results showed that EP/P-CDs1 exhibited high LOI values (31.3 %) with a UL-94 V-0 rating. The thermo-mechanical properties of EP/CDs vitrimers almost kept the same before and after reshaping, indicating good reprocessing stability. Compared with the tensile strength of original EP/P-CDs1 (38.0 MPa), the EP/P-CDs1 after one and two reprocessing still maintained a better tensile strength being 35.8 MPa and 35.4 MPa. This study would present a novel approach for designing organic-inorganic hybrid EP/CDs vitrimers composites with improved thermal and mechanical as well as flame retardancy properties.
